issues.title: PresenceBar avatar tooltips and overflow button lack ARIA attributes and keyboard access
issues.description: Avatar circles are not focusable via keyboard (no tabIndex, no role='button'). The overflow '+N' indicator has no aria-label. Tooltip content is only visible on hover with no keyboard trigger. Screen readers cannot discover who is present on the page.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/PresenceBar.tsx, make these specific changes: In the avatar rendering loop where each user circle div is rendered inside the Tooltip.Trigger, add tabIndex={0}, role="button", and aria-label={`${user.displayName} is ${getPresenceStatus(user.lastSeen)}`} to each Tooltip.Trigger element. For the overflow indicator (the '+N more' span/div), add role="status" and aria-label={`${overflow} more people viewing`}. BEFORE: Avatar circles are plain divs with no keyboard focusability and no ARIA roles; overflow count has no semantic meaning for assistive tech. AFTER: Each avatar is keyboard focusable with descriptive aria-label including the user name and status; overflow count is announced by screen readers as a live status region. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: EditConflictGuard useOptimisticLock silently blocks saves when supabase query fails without retry
issues.description: When checkConflict() encounters a Supabase error, it sets checkFailed=true and returns true (conflict detected), blocking the save. But there is no retry mechanism and the toast message says 'Please retry' without providing a retry action. The user is stuck unless they manually refresh the page.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/EditConflictGuard.tsx, in the useOptimisticLock hook's checkConflict callback, make these specific changes: After the Supabase query fails (error branch), implement a single automatic retry with a 1 second delay before setting checkFailed=true. Add a retryCount ref (max 1 retry). Update the toast to include an actionable message. BEFORE: On query error, immediately sets checkFailed=true and shows toast saying 'Please retry' with no mechanism to retry; user is permanently blocked from saving. AFTER: On first failure, waits 1 second and retries the query once. If the retry also fails, sets checkFailed=true and the toast message reads 'Could not verify edits. Refresh the page and try again.' The checkFailed state resets to false when checkConflict is called again (allowing manual retry by the consuming component). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: EntityPresence avatar buttons meet 44px touch target but PresenceDots overflow text is too small to tap
issues.description: The PresenceDots component renders the overflow count ('+N') as a plain span with no minimum touch target size. On mobile, this small text is untappable. The visible avatars have 44px buttons which is correct, but the overflow indicator needs the same treatment for consistency and field usability.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/EntityPresence.tsx, in the PresenceDots component where the overflow count is rendered (the span showing '+N'), wrap it in a button element with minWidth: 44, minHeight: 44, display: 'flex', alignItems: 'center', justifyContent: 'center', background: 'transparent', border: 'none', cursor: 'pointer', padding: 0. Add aria-label={`${overflow} more people viewing`} and role="status" to the button. BEFORE: Overflow count is a plain span element with no touch target sizing and no ARIA semantics; impossible to interact with on mobile. AFTER: Overflow count is wrapped in a 44x44px minimum touch target button with proper ARIA label, matching the avatar button pattern already used for individual user dots. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: EntityPresence trackEntity/untrackEntity calls reference unimplemented presenceStore methods
issues.description: The PresenceDots useEffect calls presenceStore.trackEntity and untrackEntity, but these methods do not exist on the store. The code defensively checks with typeof but silently falls through, meaning entity-level presence data is never actually tracked. The TODO comment confirms this is known but unresolved.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/EntityPresence.tsx, in the PresenceDots component's useEffect that calls trackEntity/untrackEntity, make these specific changes: Remove the dead code block that attempts to call trackEntity/untrackEntity since those store methods do not exist. Instead, use the existing getUsersViewingEntity selector which already reads from the presence store state populated by the parent PresenceBar's Liveblocks subscription. Add a comment explaining the data flow: presence data flows from PresenceBar's useOthers hook into presenceStore, and PresenceDots reads it via getUsersViewingEntity. BEFORE: useEffect contains dead code calling nonexistent store methods with a typeof guard that always fails silently; gives false impression that entity tracking requires explicit registration. AFTER: useEffect is removed or simplified to only handle cleanup if needed; component relies solely on the getUsersViewingEntity selector for its data, matching the actual data flow architecture. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: ConflictBanner in EditConflictGuard is declared as interface but component implementation is cut off
issues.description: The ConflictBannerProps interface is defined at the bottom of EditConflictGuard.tsx but the actual ConflictBanner component appears to be truncated (file ends after the interface declaration). This means consuming components that import ConflictBanner will get a runtime error or undefined export. The conflict warning UI that should display when optimistic lock detects a stale edit is missing.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/collaboration/EditConflictGuard.tsx, after the ConflictBannerProps interface definition at the bottom of the file, add the ConflictBanner component implementation. The component should render a warning banner with: background color colors.statusDangerBg or a light red (#FEF2F2), a row layout with AlertTriangle icon (color: colors.danger), bold text 'This item was modified by another user', a 'Refresh' button (onClick calls onRefresh) and an 'X' dismiss button (onClick calls onDismiss). Use theme tokens for all spacing and colors. Export the component. BEFORE: ConflictBannerProps interface is declared but no ConflictBanner component exists; any import of ConflictBanner fails at runtime. AFTER: ConflictBanner is a fully implemented and exported React component that renders a visible warning banner with refresh and dismiss actions, using the AlertTriangle, RefreshCw, and X icons already imported at the top of the file. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore has basic presence indicators showing who last edited a record but no real time concurrent editing awareness. Fieldwire has no collaboration features beyond comment threads. SiteSync's Liveblocks integration for real time presence is architecturally superior but the implementation has gaps: dead code paths for entity tracking, missing conflict resolution UI, and accessibility failures that would block enterprise accessibility audits. Fixing these gaps would put SiteSync's collaboration ahead of both Procore and ACC.
summary: The Collaboration & Team module has a solid architectural foundation with Liveblocks presence integration, optimistic locking via EditConflictGuard, and entity-level presence dots. However, execution gaps prevent these features from being production ready. The ConflictBanner component is incomplete (truncated file), EntityPresence references nonexistent store methods creating dead code paths, the optimistic lock has no retry mechanism leaving users permanently blocked on transient network errors, and accessibility is poor across all presence components (no keyboard navigation, missing ARIA attributes on interactive elements). Mobile touch targets are partially addressed but inconsistent. The five identified fixes target the most impactful gaps: completing the conflict UI, fixing dead code, adding error recovery, and ensuring accessibility compliance. These are all surgical fixes that do not require new features, just completing and hardening what exists.
